text: The foot is the most common anatomical site of trail running injury, followed by the knee, lower leg, thigh, and ankle.
Skin lacerations/abrasions are the most common trail running injury diagnoses, followed by skin blisters, muscle strains, muscle cramping, and ligament sprains.
Gastrointestinal tract issues are the most common illness among trail runners, followed by metabolic and cardiovascular system problems.
Nausea, vomiting related to GIT distress, and dehydration are commonly reported symptoms among trail runners during racing.
Trail running injury incidence ranges from 1.6 to 4285.0 injuries per 1000 hours of running, with the wide range reflecting differences in injury definitions and race distances.
Trail running illness incidence ranges from 65.0 to 6676.6 illnesses per 1000 hours of running.
Most trail running injury and illness research focuses on single-day race events, with very limited evidence on training-related injuries and illnesses.
